q3o0: Perhaps the most obvious aspect of effective classroom management is classroom rules and procedures. Rules and procedures convey the message that "I'm here to teach and you're here to learn." They give students the structure they need and also help them feel that the classroom is a safe and predictable place.
q7q: Teachers should focus on identified motivation—explicitly communicating the importance of learning and the value of effort—rather than chasing intrinsic motivation, which is unrealistic for most subject teachers. Schools can cultivate identified motivation and integrated motivation (the sense that effort defines who we are as a class) through explicit value communication, aspirational culture, and conditions for competence via structured teaching and unconditional positive regard.
q3oo: This approach is based on Abraham Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs theory which suggests that human beings share a wide range of emotional and psychological needs. By meeting these needs in the classroom teachers can effectively maximise student engagement while preventing behaviour problems which often arise due to feelings of boredom, frustration and alienation. We focus on three broad groups: Empowerment, Fun, and the need to Belong.
qad: Brown draws a parallel between personal exercise motivation and student motivation in classrooms. She argues that success breeds motivation rather than the reverse—students must experience intellectual success before developing intrinsic motivation to learn. Drawing on cognitive psychologist Daniel Willingham's research, Brown explains that thinking is pleasurable when it leads to genuine problem-solving. Teachers must design lessons that balance challenge with achievability, helping students experience the satisfaction of successful thinking. The essay critiques transmission models of teaching and emphasizes that authentic engagement requires students to experience real intellectual progress.
q3p3: There must be one simple answer to these questions, and that is an emphatic yes! If we do not believe it is possible to create a welcoming environment, then we really have to reexamine what we expect from our stakeholders. If they do not feel welcome, how can we expect them to participate, engage, and achieve?
qg5: Patrick Dempsey uses the airline industry as a metaphor for understanding student disengagement in higher education. While people tolerate the discomfort of flying because they value the destination, airlines still carefully choreograph the experience to make discomfort psychologically survivable. Higher education dismisses the importance of the learning experience itself, assuming that degree value excuses poor course design. Dempsey applies David Maister's psychology of waiting research to show that students' disengagement stems not from laziness but from educational experiences that are psychologically unintelligible—with unexplained waits, uncertain timelines, and disconnection from purpose.
q3ps: Learning how to manage student behavior in the classroom is one of the most challenging and important skills teachers need to master. If students ignore their teachers' lessons, disrupt the flow of instruction, disturb other students, or act rudely, they inhibit the learning of every student in the classroom, including themselves.
qgw: Belonging is linguistic, not pastoral. The mainstream literature treats belonging as an emotional microclimate that warm adults create for children to bask in or shiver at the margins of. Didau argues instead that school runs through talk at every level (lessons, corridors, feedback) carrying specific grammar, cadence, and academic assumptions. Children who can enter that discourse feel like insiders; children who cannot stay on the fringes no matter how warm the adults. Standard instruments miss this because they measure perceptions of care without examining the medium through which those perceptions form. The move is to stop treating belonging as separate from academic work, and start treating explicit teaching of academic language as a belonging intervention. Children need fluency in specialized school language, yes, but they also need to recognize themselves as members of the communities that use it. The teaching has to be explicit and the membership has to be named.
q3qc: Reflections on Student Motivation: Student motivation does not just happen; teachers need to make a conscious effort to build and sustain it. Uninspired lessons do not yield inspired learners. What motivates kids may seem counterintuitive in some ways: bribes and rewards, while happily accepted by students, do not work as well as compelling, relevant tasks given in a positive, encouraging environment.
qil: Belonging in schools depends on entry into the conceptual world of academic language, not merely on psychological identification or relatedness. While Social Identity Theory and Self-Determination Theory explain why humans seek connection, they cannot account for the cultural substance and shared meanings that define academic community. Without fluency in academic language, students may feel socially welcomed but remain outside the intellectual life of the school. Language is the medium through which the group's world becomes intelligible and full belonging becomes possible.
q3qy: Love and Logic parents react differently from other parents when kids make mistakes. We don't get angry, we don't say, 'I told you so,' and we don't lecture them about their errors. If we did those things, we would be impeding the logic of the consequences. The child's anger would be directed toward us and not toward the lesson the consequences teach. The thing that drives the lesson into our children's hearts after they make a mistake is our empathy and sadness.
qpj: Peter Gray argues that William Golding's Lord of the Flies has done lasting damage by entrenching the myth that children left unsupervised will devolve into violence and savagery. He counters with a real case recovered by historian Rutger Bregman: a group of Tongan schoolboys stranded on an uninhabited island for 15 months who cooperated throughout, resolved quarrels peacefully, kept fire alive, tended a garden, cared for an injured companion, and built a guitar from salvaged materials. Gray connects the novel's widespread assignment in schools to the foundational ideology of compulsory schooling—the assumption that children require adult authority to function—and notes that teachers who maintain classroom control are professionally rewarded regardless of whether they help children learn. He examines Golding's biography, noting the author acknowledged personal darkness (alcohol, violence, depression) and an admission of attempted rape at 18, and argues his novel reflects one man's struggle with his own demons rather than universal children's nature. Gray's conclusion is that authoritarian control more often incites than prevents children's brutality, and the Golding narrative deserves critical interrogation rather than treated as natural truth. The article closes with an invitation to readers to share whether their experience with actual children confirms or disconfirms Golding's premise.
q3s2: The hard truth is, this is the moment. Your students are at their most willing and agreeable. They want a teacher they can respect and look up to. Yet when it's time to step up, you cave. It is this moment, this pause between heartbeats when you decide to follow through or not, that separates the happiest, most effective teachers from the rest.
qpl: Peter Gray presents data from a new Institute for Family Studies survey of nearly 24,000 U.S. parents covering over 40,000 children, revealing that American children have drastically less outdoor freedom than previous generations. Fourteen-year-olds today have less freedom than 5-year-olds had a few decades ago. The data show that 58% of 10-year-olds, 27% of 14-year-olds, and 16% of 17-year-olds cannot leave their own yard without adult accompaniment. Remarkably, 62% of surveyed parents said children aged 8-12 should receive MORE supervision than they currently do -- even as most 10-year-olds are already confined to their own yard. Gray argues this level of restriction is both irrational and cruel, and that a primary effect of restricted movement is the loss of opportunity to socialize with peers outside of adult-structured settings. The findings hold across neighborhood type and socioeconomic class, suggesting this is a broad cultural phenomenon rather than a response to actual safety data. Key Points: * Survey data from nearly 24,000 U.S. parents shows that 58% of 10-year-olds and 27% of 14-year-olds cannot leave their own yard without adult accompaniment -- a level of restriction unthinkable a generation ago. * The restrictions are not correlated with actual neighborhood safety: children in EPA-rated 'walkable' neighborhoods are just as restricted as those in less walkable areas, and findings hold across socioeconomic groups. * The primary measurable effect of restricted movement is reduced time spent with other children outside of adult-controlled activities, which has downstream consequences for social development and friendship formation. * Perhaps most alarming: 62% of parents said children aged 8-12 should receive more supervision than they already do -- even as data show most 10-year-olds cannot leave their own yard without adult escort. * Gray frames this as both a societal failure (the 'chaining' metaphor) and a developmental crisis, citing previous work on how the loss of free-range childhood has damaged children's psychological and social growth.
q3u4: If the teacher doesn't provide a means to meet these needs as part of regular day-to-day practice, then students will seek satisfaction in less appropriate ways of their own devising. In other words, if you don't give them fun, they will make their own. If you don't give them a sense of empowerment, they will assert themselves in their own way. And if you don't help them feel valued, they will opt out and form troublemaking splinter groups.
qpq: In this instalment of Play Makes Us Human, Peter Gray visits a woodland free-play area near his home called 'Let Children Play LLC,' organised by Megan Devendorf, and uses his observations to illustrate several of his longstanding arguments about child-directed play. The key scene is preschool-age children carefully and persistently climbing a boulder to access a plastic slide -- demonstrating natural caution, iterative risk assessment, and persistent self-directed effort -- while parents sat on blankets without hovering or intervening. Gray observes that adult non-intervention is not neglect but trust: children are remarkably competent in play, and adult over-involvement prevents them from manifesting that competence. The most memorable moment involves an 8-year-old who noticed the younger children struggling, retrieved a ladder from the loose-parts pile unprompted, and made it available -- an example of age-mixed play producing spontaneous prosocial behaviour without adult prompting. For Gray, this illustrates his consistent claim: age-mixed, child-directed outdoor play in natural settings produces social learning, physical risk competence, and mutual care that supervised, age-segregated, adult-managed play cannot replicate.
q3ur: There are teachers who make great and exciting and inspiring lessons out of every topic that some other teacher considers a grind. Our job is to find a way to make what we teach engaging and never to assume that students can't appreciate what's not instantly familiar. A belief that content is boring is a self-fulfilling prophecy.
qxw: Melinda Karshner, a veteran literacy specialist, argues that the science of reading and love of reading are not in conflict -- but that bloated basal readers may be in conflict with both, when teachers are not given room to supplement them with whole-class novels. Citing a podcast conversation with Timothy Shanahan, she draws the key distinction between instructional reading (what the basal is for) and the wider range of books students should read -- basals are what teachers teach with, not all that students should experience. Her own practice replaces most basal excerpts with complete, curriculum-aligned novels: Frindle at year's start (covering character change, point of view, figurative language, and author's purpose while hooking students on morphology), with the basal then used for its proper purpose -- targeted skill instruction and transfer checking. She advocates for cross-curricular integration (reading, social studies, science, and writing woven around module themes), arguing that knowledge-building drives reading comprehension and that this integration makes both teaching and student engagement stronger. The article's bottom line is a 'both/and' -- the basal does its instructional job, teachers do theirs -- and it is the space between, where teacher judgment about text selection lives, that often gets left out of instructional decisions.
q3vg: He also teaches students to identify important information they'll need to solve the problem. By teaching students to organize their work clearly, he sets them up to succeed not just in his class but in future math classes.
q13j: Linsin's perennial classroom-management argument that 'friendship energy' from teacher to student causes a cascading set of management problems and undermines the very respect-and-warmth relationship teachers are reaching for when they try to be friends. The piece distinguishes between the mature, kind, professional regard that students need from a teacher and the peer-style friendliness that erodes classroom authority. When teachers chase friendship, students stop reading the structure of the room as stable; behaviors that should be quietly redirected become repeated, and the teacher pays for the informality with louder corrections later. Linsin's positive frame: students do not need a friend (they have those), they need a calm, predictable, fair, dignified adult who likes them, holds the line, and teaches them well. The relational warmth a teacher should bring is not friendship; it's the warmth of a trusted adult who uses authority on the student's behalf. Note: this PDF was reconstructed from the email header and the original archived PDF text; the source email arrived as HTML-only with no plaintext body, so the underlying article should be re-fetched directly from smartclassroommanagement.com if a fuller rebuild is needed.
q3vq: It's smart to add details rather than making it too simple. Challenge is good and will increase concentration, improve memory, and keep your students focused and purpose-driven from the moment they walk through the door.
q13o: All students want to like their teacher, yet research shows most do not. The problem is not rooted in personality mismatch or inherent student dislike, but rather in how teachers approach relationships and structure classroom culture. Students like and respect teachers who demonstrate clear boundaries, follow through consistently on every promise, show genuine and visible care for their growth as people, and create an environment of safety and procedural fairness. Building positive student-teacher relationships requires intentional, deliberate effort to communicate reliability, consistency, and authentic interest in students as whole individuals—not merely as learners. Teachers must signal that they remember what matters to students, act predictably, and deliver on commitments. When these conditions are met, even initially reserved students warm to their teachers.
q3x1: Furthermore, research shows that when visual stimuli are chaotic, children tend to behave chaotically. A clutter-filled room is distracting and stressful. It interferes with their sense of safety and well-being. It creates excitability, which is one of the biggest causes of misbehavior.
q14v: Katie Martin argues that the chronic absenteeism gripping American schools is not a compliance problem but a design problem — a signal that the traditional school model has decoupled from students' lives, identities, and futures. Drawing on personal experience, educator testimony, and the research framework from The Disengaged Teen, she identifies four student archetypes — Resisters, Passengers, Achievers, and Explorers — and argues that schools are inadvertently producing the first three. The article traces how the erosion of meaningful school experiences (field days, traditions, community rituals), combined with the rise of AI that can complete assignments and enable high grades without engagement, has rendered the case for attendance increasingly fragile. Martin distinguishes between disengagement from learning and disengagement from the system, insisting young people are not lazy but are responding rationally to systems that feel irrelevant and disconnected. She calls on educators and school designers to stop layering more compliance onto a broken model and instead rebuild schools into environments worthy of student presence.
q3xh: Creating the climate and environment conducive for learning is imperative for intrinsic motivation. As previously stated, brains have to be safe in order to be fully engaged and to avoid reverting to the fight, flight, or freeze mode. Students need to feel that they have unconditional regard and are respected.
q17a: This article by an Australian school leader argues that instructional leadership -- deep knowledge of best practice in teaching and learning -- must be non-optional for school leaders. The author observes a cruel irony at the heart of education: the more expert a practitioner becomes, the more likely they are to be promoted into leadership roles that remove them from the classroom. Leadership silos further compound the problem, as each portfolio holder becomes an island, losing sight of the ways everything in education is interconnected -- student management, pedagogy, inclusion, literacy, numeracy, digital learning, and wellbeing all overlap in a Venn diagram of mutual influence. Using the anecdote of a principal who called 'checking for understanding' 'Shaun's Checks for Understanding,' the author illustrates how even a year of school-wide implementation can pass without the building's top leader developing real knowledge of the practice being implemented. The solution proposed includes professional reading, school research leads, leadership book clubs, and rotating leadership portfolios to prevent single-portfolio expertise without broader system awareness.
q3xr: Education built around the notion of listening objects or stand-and-deliver teaching is not effective for young minds, and it doesn't work for adults either. At any age, people need to pause and process what they're learning. They need to chew on concepts, jot down their thoughts, compare understandings with peers, and articulate their questions.
q17n: This article from the Mutually Assured Instruction newsletter examines the gap between teachers' initial reluctance to adopt explicit instruction (EI) and what happens after they give it a genuine try -- and why even enthusiastic early adopters often fail to sustain the shift into full implementation. The author catalogs the familiar straw-man arguments against explicit instruction (students need to discover, need to struggle, won't be engaged) and notes a recurring pattern: teachers who trial one element (mini whiteboards, a Do Now, checks for understanding) report dramatically positive student responses and become relatively converted. The catch is the word 'relatively.' The article distinguishes between treating explicit instruction as a 'toolbelt' (novelties to deploy occasionally) versus understanding it as a consolidating system of routines that becomes more effective through consistency. 'Consistency over novelty' is the article's spine: the phrase 'I'll use mini whiteboards every now and then' misunderstands why the technique works, which is precisely because routines build predictability into which deeper learning can happen. The article also examines the 'explicit instruction portion of the lesson' misconception -- the idea that EI occupies one segment of a lesson -- and argues this is a category error: explicit instruction is the entire architecture from Do Now through recap to new learning through checks for understanding, with scaffolding continuously fading toward independence throughout.
q3y3: PRINCIPLE FIVE: IT ALL POINTS TO HEALTHY RELATIONSHIPS. Will this intervention build or maintain a relationship with the students where I am viewed as powerful and compassionate at the very same time? Am I remembering that challenging students need strong teachers who are friendly… not friendly teachers who are pushovers?
q17p: A proposal to rebrand short-cycle formative assessment using the acronym I.C.E. (Instruct, Check, Evaluate), drawing parallels to ice therapy in sports. The post advocates for educators to implement this framework repeatedly throughout lessons, applying it as frequently as ice is applied to an injury.
q3yk: I begin this book with the "Good" Kids, and that's where I start when I face a new group of students each year — they're all good kids, but I cannot be so naive as to think that they come to me without a past. A class is not a concrete unit but a room full of individuals who may have to be reached in different ways.
q180: An Australian educator examines why student belonging surveys in public schools consistently reveal disconnection and argues that popular school initiatives aimed at building belonging are fundamentally ineffective. The author surveys five common approaches -- fundraising embarrassments, free BBQs, pastoral care classes, themed cause days, and reward systems -- and shows each fails because it is tokenistic, disconnected from students' actual interests, or actively counterproductive. By contrast, the author's experiences in American and independent Australian schools reveal that genuine belonging emerges only when extracurricular participation is structurally embedded in the school day, making every student feel pride, connection, and daily success. The public school system's inability to codify this in teacher conditions leaves belonging as a goal endlessly chased with gestures that change nothing. The article concludes that high-quality teaching and consistent high expectations are the most effective belonging strategy available: students connect with places that make them feel safe and capable.
q3z1: If we make it clear ahead of time that we expect students to be prepared, responsible, and on time, then we can start out on the right foot and keep moving forward. Great teachers establish clear expectations at the start of the year and follow them consistently as the year progresses.
q184: This article critiques restorative practice programs -- particularly Real Schools, Australia's largest such provider -- arguing that restorative conversations are fundamentally reactive, targeting behaviour after it occurs rather than preventing it. The author's central contention is that a developmentally honest understanding of adolescent neurology (pre-frontal cortex immaturity until mid-twenties) makes post-hoc reflection an inherently weak behaviour management lever: teenagers need firm, supportive structures applied consistently, not reflective conversations after violations have already occurred. The piece scrutinises Real Schools' data claims, noting that pre/post survey improvements in 'school climate' and 'effective classroom behaviour' are self-reported and collected by the very teachers implementing the program -- a textbook observer-expectation confound. The author draws a distinction between proactive behaviour management systems that prevent misbehaviour through clear expectations and routines, and reactive systems that respond to it, arguing that well-designed proactive systems make expensive consultant-dependent reactive programs largely unnecessary. The article acknowledges that restorative conversations have legitimate uses for maintaining long-term relationships, but insists that for teenagers in school settings, structure, predictability, and early prevention are the load-bearing elements of behaviour management.
q3zr: Use a more positive approach. With any classroom problem there are two ways of approaching it — reward positive behaviour or punish inappropriate behaviour. Rather than focusing on students who don't bring equipment, it might be better to reward those who do.
q187: A practical case for chunking — breaking instruction into small, cognitively manageable pieces — as foundational to effective teaching. Human working memory can hold only 3–7 novel items before becoming overwhelmed, which means teachers who present too much content at once virtually guarantee that students will retain nothing. Chunking is not merely dividing content into segments; it requires understanding the 'exploded diagram' of a concept and sequencing components so each builds on the last. The author walks through a concrete example from VCE Business Management (inputs, processes, outputs) showing how a single standard expands into approximately 15 teachable sub-steps within a 60-minute lesson. Crucially, chunking is only half the battle — checking for understanding within each chunk is what converts sequenced exposure into retained learning. Since so much of teaching is sequential, each piece building on the prior one, failing to chunk properly means building without a foundation: the structure looks fine until assessment pressure exposes what was never actually learned. Key Takeaways • Human working memory holds 3–7 novel items before becoming overwhelmed; instruction must be deliberately chunked to avoid exceeding this capacity and losing learning entirely. • Chunking is inherently sequential — each piece must build on prior knowledge; teaching without this foundation creates apparent learning that collapses under assessment. • Checking for understanding within each chunk is what distinguishes chunking that produces durable learning from chunking that merely sequences exposure. • New teachers struggle to chunk effectively because expertise is required to see all the small parts that compose a larger concept — novices can't decompose what they can't yet fully see. • Even a 'simple' single-standard lesson point should decompose into ~15 sub-steps with multiple response opportunities per step; a 60-minute class at face value gives students only ~8 chances to respond, far fewer than optimal.
q409: The climate of a classroom refers to the type of environment students are exposed to when they are learning. Freiberg (1998) believes that school climate is an ever-changing factor in the lives of the students and teachers and states that "much like the air we breathe, school climate is ignored until it becomes foul."
q18c: This Strat Chat installment focuses on teacher circulation -- deliberate, purposeful movement around the classroom -- as a high-leverage instructional strategy. Using biological and economic metaphors, the author argues that stagnation is harmful while deliberate movement distributes what is needed. In teaching, circulation involves strategic positioning during instruction, independent work, and checks for understanding. The piece argues that where a teacher stands and moves sends powerful signals about responsibility for learning and the teacher's conception of their role. Effective circulation enables teachers to gather low-stakes formative data, redirect students privately, and maintain accountability without constant whole-class interruption. The Strat Chat format provides a tight, practical focus: one strategy explained clearly with direct application to classroom practice.
q40t: Now that your room arrangement planning has prepared the physical space of the classroom, you must consider how you will organize students' social space. An effective classroom has patterns and routines in place that make interaction and movement easy to organize and accomplish. For students to have a successful year in your classroom, they must understand and practice the behaviors you expect of them.
q18j: This Mutually Assured Instruction post makes the case that school-wide consistency in classroom routines dramatically reduces cognitive load for students and improves behavior. When every teacher sets different routines, students must 'code switch' between five or more sets of expectations per day -- a hidden tax on working memory that falls hardest on students with additional needs. The author argues that effective routines share common features: teacher present before students, a visual schedule, a Do Now activity, a recap of prior learning, and consistent expectations for participation throughout. The post draws on research showing that predictable environments reduce anxiety and stress, and closes with a behavioral principle: routines must be reinforced relentlessly over time because the brain defaults to the path of least resistance.
q41e: As discussed earlier, effective classroom managers are aware of the unique needs of individual students, particularly those from challenging backgrounds. Although many teachers sense such needs instinctively, it is useful to formally identify categories of high-need students and the management strategies that are most effective within each category.
q18o: An Australian secondary teacher diagnoses 'results inflation' -- the systemic tendency for student grades to be reported at or above curriculum level regardless of actual achievement -- and identifies four interlocking causes: the near-impossibility of having a student repeat a year, parental expectation of constant upward progress, teacher ignorance of what curriculum levels actually mean, and the implicit punishment of teachers whose students fail. The author documents the downstream harm: students who arrive at senior years with 80s on their transcripts unable to perform at that level, and teachers who follow the spec being questioned while colleagues who inflate face no scrutiny. The piece concludes that teachers are not the problem -- the systemic incentives are -- and calls for reform of reporting norms and teacher education on curriculum standards.
q41x: I believe passionately and wholeheartedly that meeting students' innate psychological needs, particularly in terms of building positive relationships with them and acknowledging their efforts, is fundamental to gaining their trust and respect. From there you can teach them, help them grow, influence them in positive ways and help them succeed.
q1g4: Dominic Salles launches a practical behavior management series by presenting a single high-leverage technique that any teacher—even an NQT on their first day—can use immediately with immediate effect: narrating what you see. After giving a clear written instruction and waiting 30 seconds, the teacher walks the rows calling out line counts aloud (3 lines, 1 line, 0 lines) without commentary, praise, or engagement. The technique creates social comparison that students respond to faster and more reliably than teacher-imposed expectations, because peer norms are far more powerful than adult authority. No one wants to appear last or least productive in front of their peers, so behavior improves without relationship capital, emotional regulation, trauma-informed practice, or any other skill. Salles is explicit that he is not dismissing those things—they matter—but this is a toolbox, not a character-development guide. The technique works because it's anonymous (everyone gets counted), it's mathematical (not evaluative), and its effect is cumulative: repeated twice or three times, it installs a new social norm where writing more is the default expectation driven by peers, not the teacher.
q42e: Within only a few minutes, we can determine the pulse of the learning environment. The good news is that classroom teachers determine the learning environment in their classrooms. Every action and inaction establishes the learning environment as ultimately positive, neutral, or negative.
q1g8: Dominic Salles makes a practical case for the classroom visualiser (document camera) as an underappreciated behavior and learning tool that requires no personal skill or charisma to deploy effectively. Unlike a slideshow, the visualiser mirrors exactly what students have in front of them, eliminating split attention, making teacher instructions visible and permanent, and creating unavoidable accountability. A random-selection protocol -- rolling a die or counting from a named student -- ensures every student knows they may be called on at any moment, replacing diffuse monitoring with universal expectation. Salles specifies that feedback displayed under the visualiser should be limited to 1-3 criteria per task to remain actionable, and that work should be displayed from the very first writing task of every lesson. The approach builds a triple reward system: correct work earns pride, teacher validation, and peer benefit; mistaken work receives immediate feedback with dignity preserved through randomness. The visualiser must be set up before registration every day to establish the routine.
q43b: This teacher had returned to the classroom thinking that all the wonderful theories, acronyms and case studies he had carefully explained in his book would help him swiftly brush aside any of the classroom problems he was likely to encounter. But he had forgotten one of the most important principles of successful teaching: students will always respond more positively to a teacher they know, like and trust.
q1nt: This is the sixth installment in Kevin Stinehart's 'Architecture of Childhood' series, examining executive function -- the brain's 'air traffic control system' -- and the conditions required for children to develop it. Stinehart opens with the familiar teacher experience of end-of-day chaos (lost shoes, misplaced folders) and argues that what looks like irresponsibility is underdeveloped executive function: the cluster of cognitive capacities including planning, attention regulation, working memory, and impulse control that develop slowly through practice, not reminders or color-coded folders. The article distinguishes between what adults call 'responsibility' and what neuroscience identifies as executive function, arguing that shame and adult over-management actively prevent its development. Executive function grows through unstructured play (where children must manage real social situations with real low-stakes consequences), movement, sleep, real responsibilities, and planning projects. The article makes the case that unstructured play provides far richer executive function training than any classroom lesson because children must regulate themselves while emotionally activated and socially involved. The piece closes with a comparison of two pathways: one child who gets the inputs that build executive function, and another whose inputs are crowded out by rushing, adult over-management, and shame.
q43s: As a culture shaper, language helps us to direct attention and action. However, the words and structures that make up language not only convey an explicit surface meaning but also impart a set of deeper associations and connections that implicitly shape thought and influence behavior. This is the hidden power of language: its ability to subtly convey messages that shape our thinking, sense of self, and group affinity.
q1nz: Kevin Stinehart argues that children today are more constantly managed, scheduled, and supervised than any generation in human history -- and that this adult saturation is systematically depriving them of the inputs that build genuine capability: choice, responsibility, risk, unstructured time, and problems to solve with other children. The piece is the third in his 'Architecture of Childhood' series and illustrates its argument through a Play Club scene where a boy struggling to build a cardboard structure discovers ownership rather than gratitude -- the look of 'I did this,' not 'someone helped me.' Stinehart argues that adults confuse constant management with good parenting, and that children who never practice independence do not suddenly become independent when adulthood arrives. He offers concrete examples of how agency grows in the home (child makes part of dinner, sweeps, carries breakable items) and at school (students manage materials, lead routines, solve small peer problems). The core claim is that systems are designed to get the results they get: a childhood designed around adult management produces managed, not capable, children.
q45a: Some students, after all is said and done, must be allowed to choose failure. However, there is a difference between reality, we won't reach everyone, and belief, we work each day as if today will be the breakthrough. It is important that we access and sustain optimism so that we can continually persist in making it difficult for our students to fail our class or themselves.
q1o6: Kevin Stinehart opens with a scene from his school's Play Club in which children spontaneously create a construction site, archaeological dig, and international border dispute from dirt and sticks -- practicing executive function, emotional regulation, and conflict repair without any adult planning. This scene anchors his larger argument: that critical developmental capacities are not choices children make but skills built through thousands of ordinary experiences, and that modern childhood has accidentally depleted those experiences. He introduces an 'Architecture of Childhood' framework identifying developmental inputs (sleep, movement, play, risk, responsibility, conversation) and the modern blockers (screens, overscheduling, adult over-control) that crowd them out. Stinehart argues the problem is not individual bad parenting but a supply chain of structural forces, including liability fears, recess cuts, and community designs that leave children no places to find each other. The essay closes with practical recommendations for every child-serving institution and an equity lens on who actually has access to these developmental conditions.
q45p: Rather than telling students, in theory-building classrooms 'the answer is never just given.' Your role becomes one of support and clarification for students' ideas rather than only validating students' responses. This transfers much authority away from you and places it on students. Students move from being passive listeners to active participants in the creation of meaning.
q1of: Kevin Stinehart's seventh piece in the Architecture of Childhood series argues that physical health and motor coordination are foundational to childhood development, not supplementary to it. Drawing on developmental neuroscience, he contends that the body is the primary instrument through which young children construct their understanding of space, causality, and social relationships -- physical competence and cognitive competence are deeply entangled, not parallel tracks. Stinehart distinguishes between gross motor development (balance, coordination, whole-body movement) and fine motor development (precision grip, tool use, hand-eye coordination), arguing both are time-sensitive: critical periods in early childhood wire the neural architecture for these capacities, and deficits incurred during these windows are difficult to remediate later. He is critical of contemporary childhood environments that restrict physical activity in the name of safety -- over-structured playgrounds, removal of risky play, sedentary screen time -- arguing these deprive children of stimulation their developing nervous systems require. The argument is developmental, not nostalgic: modern childhoods are neurologically impoverished, not merely less fun. Stinehart closes with practical implications: prioritize unstructured outdoor play, tolerate appropriate physical risk, treat physical education as cognitive investment, and evaluate screen time not only for content but for the developmental displacement it represents.
q45z: In order to get control of our emotional reactions so that we can respond instead, we must take two counterintuitive action steps: Choose not to take their behavior personally, even if it is. Don't waste your emotional oxygen huffing and puffing about your kids' behavior.
q1oh: Kevin Stinehart uses a school garden metaphor — the difference between jet, mist, and shower hose settings — to argue that modern schools have been set to 'Jet': they treat education as a delivery problem (instructional minutes, standards covered, interventions administered) while almost entirely ignoring the absorption gap between what adults deliver and what children's brains, bodies, and nervous systems can actually take in, hold, and use. Using the American Academy of Pediatrics' 2026 recess guidance as a launching point, Stinehart argues that recess is not dessert but digestion: the place where learning metabolises from 'I understood that while the teacher was standing next to me' into durable, applicable knowledge. Withholding recess as punishment is a design flaw, not a discipline strategy — the children most in need of movement, peer practice, and stress regulation are consistently the first to lose it. The article ends with the proposition that 'recovery is the new rigor': genuine rigour requires designing conditions where children can sustain meaningful effort without breakdown.
q46f: Once you have an understanding of what they truly need it becomes not so difficult after all. You realize that they're just kids in need of direction and recalibration. They're kids in need of someone to restore their dignity and switch on their intrinsic motivational engines. That someone is you.
q1op: Stinehart argues that children's resilience and grit are not innate traits but emergent capacities that grow from deliberate exposure to age-appropriate difficulty, failure, and recovery — and that modern childhood has systematically removed the inputs that build them. The central paradox: adults say they want resilient children but constantly remove the small, survivable challenges that build resilience — rushing in when children feel frustrated, smoothing paths so they rarely fail, and treating ordinary difficulty as evidence that something has gone wrong. The article uses the vivid illustration of Stinehart watching his daughters build a collapsing fort: the repeated failures, accusations, and returns to try again are not signs of distress but of the developmental work that produces persistence and problem-solving. Stinehart draws on systems improvement science to reframe the issue: systems are designed to get the results they get — childhood that is overprotected from ordinary hardship is designed to produce fragile children, whether or not that was anyone's intention. The key distinction is between support and rescue: children need an adult nearby as a resource, but that adult must hold back unless genuine safety is at risk, because no one was in danger and the struggle itself was the point. This is Part 4 of Stinehart's 'Architecture of Childhood' series, which has previously covered independence/agency, social connection, and creativity/imagination.
q46v: Most teachers are overwhelmed with work. They're rushed and breathless. They're forever behind and straining to catch up. It's not possible to be an effective teacher this way. It's not possible to influence and inspire students when you're not at your best, when you're just trying to keep your head above water.
q1ot: This article introduces 'The Architecture of Childhood' — a framework of nine developmental inputs children need to grow into regulated, capable, resilient adults — and uses self-regulation as the first detailed case study. Kevin Stinehart argues that self-regulation is not a personality trait some children happen to have but a capacity built through thousands of small developmental experiences: manageable stress, free play, boredom, physical movement, unstructured time, and the daily practice of waiting, choosing, negotiating, and recovering from frustration. The article's central provocation is that modern childhood has systematically pushed out precisely the inputs that build regulation — replacing free play with screens, packing schedules tight, rescuing children from boredom and discomfort, and removing risk — and then expresses shock when children can't regulate. Stinehart draws on the American Academy of Pediatrics and Harvard's Center on the Developing Child to show that executive function and self-regulation must be built through experience, not taught directly. The dysregulated child, the piece concludes, is often the receipt for the childhood design the adults around them chose.
q478: Effective classroom management is about action. It's about doing something in response to misbehavior. It's about communicating what you want then holding students accountable for doing it. Resolve to stop being so disappointed by whole-class misbehavior. Instead, start looking at them as opportunities to make your class better, sharper, and more receptive to your instruction.
q1ow: Kevin Stinehart argues that the defining paradox of modern childhood is that children are surrounded by people all day and still feel profoundly alone. Supervised, scheduled, and entertained constantly, they lack what actually builds social competence: free, unstructured peer interaction -- time to talk, wander, argue, repair, forgive badly, and slowly figure out how to belong. This is the second essay in his 'Architecture of Childhood' series, which frames child development in terms of inputs and outputs: the capacities (empathy, trust, communication, belonging) we want in children require the specific conditions that build them. Stinehart makes a particular claim about friendship: it is not enrichment but a 'load-bearing wall' of development, and we are systematically dismantling it by filling childhood with adult-managed activities at the expense of genuine peer relationships. The case is not anti-activity, but pro-unstructured time -- a distinction parents and institutions alike tend to collapse.
q47o: The problem with most instructions is that the means of participation comes at the end. By that point, students' attention has turned to the question and most have stopped listening. Front-load the means of participation to ensure students can pay attention to how you want them to respond. This saves time and leads to a much calmer, more focused classroom.
q1p9: Schools inadvertently damage gifted students by creating educational environments where capability and challenge no longer align. When instruction moves too slowly for advanced learners, students begin to doubt their own abilities because effort no longer produces success. This erodes the gifted child's sense of intellectual identity and capability. The result is students who were genuinely gifted but no longer believe themselves to be, whose self-concept has been damaged by years of insufficient challenge. The danger isn't just underachievement—it's the internalization of low expectations that students carry forward. Schools have a responsibility to create appropriately challenging learning experiences for advanced students, not as enrichment add-ons, but as core instructional responsibility.
q3o1: Research clearly supports the notion that designing and implementing rules and procedures in class significantly influences students' behavior and learning. But research also indicates that rules and procedures should not simply be imposed on students; they should be created with students. Effective teachers take the time to explain the reasons behind particular rules and procedures and involve students in creating them.
q1pf: Documents how a simple idea—unstructured play time without adult intervention—transformed a South Carolina school's culture and sparked a statewide movement. In fall 2019, the author's elementary school began 'Play Club': 90 minutes weekly of free play with a single rule: no purposely hurting anyone. Adults stayed back, watching like lifeguards. Within weeks, children self-organized, conflicts resolved without intervention, and behavioral referrals plummeted from 200+ annually to under 50. Research showed Play Club participants outperformed waitlist peers on state reading and math tests. The story gained attention through Jon Haidt's book 'The Anxious Generation,' attracting interest from the U.S. Surgeon General and leading South Carolina to become the first Let Grow Lighthouse State, officially promoting unstructured play statewide.
q3op: If the teacher doesn't provide a means to meet these needs as part of regular day-to-day practice, students will seek satisfaction in less appropriate ways of their own devising. In other words, if you don't give them fun, they'll make their own. If you don't give them a sense of power, they will assert themselves in their own way. And if you don't make them feel valued they will likely opt out and form trouble-making splinter groups.
q1pn: Stinehart uses a parking lot observation -- carts piled on curbs at a store with few corrals, all returned at the adjacent store with abundant corrals -- to argue that school behavior problems are systems problems, not character problems. Drawing on education systems improvement science (Carnegie Foundation) and developmental research on play, he contends that schools have removed the inputs (recess, unstructured play, movement) that build executive function -- the very capacities they then demand from students throughout the day. His school's Play Club reduced physical aggression incidents from 65 to 32 and cut office referrals for defiance in half. The cruelest irony: schools punish dysregulated students by removing recess -- taking away the developmental tool that would build the regulation they lack. Stinehart closes with practical diagnostic questions: where does behavior fall apart, what have we asked children to do in the hour before, and what does our schedule make 'easy' versus 'hard'?
q3p4: We believe that school leaders must observe, model, coach, and expect this from each member of the school community. We believe systems can be designed and put in place to elicit these types of behaviors. The effect on students is monumental. When students are immersed in a welcoming culture, they in turn sustain and extend that welcome to others.
q1pu: Dave Stuart Jr. shares a letter from a reader named Mandy, a Brooklyn high school English teacher who survived a challenging year of transition by grounding herself in his Five Key Beliefs methodology (Credibility, Effort, Efficacy, Belonging, Value). Mandy's letter is a practitioner's testimony to how a philosophical framework—not just a set of tactics—can provide stability when teaching conditions are destabilizing. Her most pressing challenge was students stuck in the lower levels of motivation (the 'red and orange sections of the Rainbow of Why'): students who had concluded that school had nothing to offer them in the world they were growing into. She connected this directly to AI: students using AI for everything because the rules seem no longer relevant and nobody agrees on new ones. Her response was a series of 'mini-sermons' about why school matters—how reading, writing, and discussion teach students to notice, to think clearly, to resist manipulation, to understand others. She describes designing an opening unit around 'School: why bother?' built on Everest statements and motivational readings. Stuart frames her letter as a model for 'inhabiting the role of teacher' rather than deploying a toolset—the deeper purpose of the Five Key Beliefs work.
q3pt: If students ignore their teachers' lessons, disrupt the flow of instruction, disturb other students, or act rudely, they inhibit the learning of every student in the classroom, including themselves. And the side effects of teaching a chaotic class can be devastating.
q1px: Dave Stuart Jr. shares a case study from Mandy, a Brooklyn English teacher who ran a 'School: Why Bother?' mini-unit at the start of the school year, culminating in collaboratively constructed Everest statements (class mission statements) for each period. The mini-unit, Stuart argues, activates three key motivational factors from his book The Will to Learn: Value (students explore why school matters), Efficacy (articulating a vision of success strengthens belief in reaching it), and Belonging (student voices become central from day one). Mandy's feedback reveals a striking result: the class that developed a strong collective mission became her favourite to teach -- 'willing to wrestle with big ideas, to be vulnerable, and to take risks' -- while the other class remained more guarded. The case study illustrates how beginning-of-year practices that treat school's value as a genuine question -- not a given -- create conditions for deeper engagement all year. Stuart closes by inviting teachers to try this approach and signals Mandy plans to expand it by having students co-create class AI policies.
q1q4: Dave Stuart Jr. uses Rocky's persistent fear of being a 'bum' as a mirror for the self-doubt many teachers harbor. Just as Rocky obsesses over proof of his worth despite his efforts and intentions, teachers often internalize self-criticism and doubt about their effectiveness, focusing on outcomes they cannot fully control. Stuart argues that a professional and honest attempt to help students grow—focusing on controllable process and effort rather than uncontrollable outcomes—is sufficient evidence that a teacher is doing meaningful work. The piece offers simple reassurance rooted in locus of control theory: teachers who invest genuine effort in their craft are not failures, regardless of whether every student achieves at the highest level.
q1qf: Stuart maps Dan Pink's four types of regret (Foundation, Boldness, Moral, Connection) onto his Five Key Beliefs (Credibility, Value, Effort, Efficacy, Belonging), arguing that regret is diagnostic information about what teachers value most: helping students learn. Foundation regrets reveal wishes to build stronger systems and expertise, Boldness regrets signal curriculum risks left untaken, Moral regrets expose moments when hurried judgment betrayed core values, and Connection regrets point to unattended relationships. Stuart offers Pink's three-step process for turning regret into action: relive it briefly, reframe it as learning about what you value, and extract one concrete change for the next term, positioning regret as a forward-facing signal rather than self-flagellation.
q200: This article argues that boundary-testing and rule-challenging behavior in adolescents is not a disciplinary failure but a developmental necessity — the natural expression of the autonomy drive that research identifies as a core psychological need during adolescence. Richard Wheadon uses the analogy of school fences: we do not build fences because we think children are broken or disobedient; we build them because children run. In the same way, schools need behavior systems not because they expect failure but because they understand how humans develop. Drawing on Self-Determination Theory (Deci and Ryan), Steinberg's developmental psychology, and Lewin's person-environment model, the article makes the case that defiance at adolescence is not an anomaly to be eliminated but a predictable feature to be designed around. The most effective behavior systems, Wheadon concludes, assume students are still learning to make decisions and provide the clear, consistent structure within which that learning can happen safely — the fence that makes the running possible.
q203: A Year 9 student's candid explanation — that he only completes homework for teachers who actually follow through with detentions — becomes the entry point for a wide-ranging argument about what really changes behavior in schools. Richard Wheadon draws on B.F. Skinner's operant conditioning experiments and criminologist Daniel Nagin's deterrence research to make the case that predictability, not severity, is the key driver of behavioral change. Skinner's Skinner Box experiments demonstrated that consistent consequences stabilized behavior while inconsistent ones produced erratic, calculating responses — exactly the pattern students display when some teachers enforce rules and others do not. Nagin's 2013 review of deterrence studies reached the same conclusion from decades of criminology research: people change their behavior based on the certainty of consequence, not its harshness. Wheadon extends this to three everyday examples — average-speed cameras, random breath testing for drink-driving, and parking enforcement — each of which shows that behavioral change follows the perceived inevitability of being caught, not the size of the penalty. Applied to schools: when students know that a consequence will happen, behavior becomes a routine; when they're uncertain, it becomes a gamble. Individual acts of compassion by teachers who bend the rules — however well-intentioned — collectively erode the system's predictability and thereby harm the very students the compassion was meant to help.
q3gx: Stuart reflects on the power and transformative nature of secret service—acts of kindness, help, and care given without recognition or expectation of return. In cultures obsessed with visibility, credit, and personal brand building, secret service is countercultural and profoundly transformative both for giver and receiver. Leaders who practice and encourage secret service build cultures of genuine generosity where people help others not for recognition or advancement but because it is right and needed. Secret service strengthens both the giver and receiver in ways visible service cannot.
q7r: Teachers should focus on identified motivation—explicitly communicating the importance of learning and the value of effort—rather than chasing intrinsic motivation, which is unrealistic for most subject teachers. Schools can cultivate identified motivation and integrated motivation (the sense that effort defines who we are as a class) through explicit value communication, aspirational culture, and conditions for competence via structured teaching and unconditional positive regard.
qae: Brown draws a parallel between personal exercise motivation and student motivation in classrooms. She argues that success breeds motivation rather than the reverse—students must experience intellectual success before developing intrinsic motivation to learn. Drawing on cognitive psychologist Daniel Willingham's research, Brown explains that thinking is pleasurable when it leads to genuine problem-solving. Teachers must design lessons that balance challenge with achievability, helping students experience the satisfaction of successful thinking. The essay critiques transmission models of teaching and emphasizes that authentic engagement requires students to experience real intellectual progress.
qg6: Patrick Dempsey uses the airline industry as a metaphor for understanding student disengagement in higher education. While people tolerate the discomfort of flying because they value the destination, airlines still carefully choreograph the experience to make discomfort psychologically survivable. Higher education dismisses the importance of the learning experience itself, assuming that degree value excuses poor course design. Dempsey applies David Maister's psychology of waiting research to show that students' disengagement stems not from laziness but from educational experiences that are psychologically unintelligible—with unexplained waits, uncertain timelines, and disconnection from purpose.
q481: In this sense, the social relates to the cognitive to the extent that behaviour, being at the root of consciousness, has a "social component": we learn not just from so-called "direct experience" with Nature, but also from the experience of others, who, like ourselves, have acquired knowledge as the result of the experience of previous generations ("historical experience"), that is not transmitted by heredity.
q0: Assessment is not merely a measurement act but a construction act: every time pupils receive test results, they internalize signals about competence, comparison, and futures. Form (pass/fail, bands, percentiles) shapes self-concept; pupils describe themselves in its terms. Context makes the same score feel triumphant or shameful depending on set placement. Schools attach moral worth to success, so struggle reads as a verdict on character. Teachers are game designers: pupils who repeatedly lose internalize loser identities and behave accordingly, driven either toward instrumental optimization of the system or toward deliberate avoidance. Assessment choices have consequences for who pupils become.
q488: Students learn more readily when they can connect what they are learning to what they already know. However, instructors should not assume that students will immediately or naturally draw on relevant prior knowledge. Instead, they should deliberately activate students' prior knowledge to help them forge robust links to new knowledge.
qc: This article applies Karl Maton's Legitimation Code Theory -- specifically his concept of semantic gravity -- to assessment design, arguing that the choice of context in an assessment question is not neutral but determines what cognitive move students must perform and which students are structurally advantaged. Semantic gravity describes the degree to which knowledge is tied to a specific context (high) or abstracted from context (low). A contextualised question (force applied to a shopping trolley) requires students to perform context-stripping before applying abstract knowledge -- an additional cognitive move that is itself a teachable skill, not a natural advantage. The article introduces 'semantic waves' -- the pedagogically productive oscillation between abstract principles and concrete examples. Good teaching rides semantic waves deliberately: moving from concept to context and back. Assessment designers should understand which position on the semantic gravity spectrum their tasks occupy and what cognitive move they are actually testing. The practical implication is that teachers should explicitly teach context-stripping as a skill. Students who have been taught to recognise the abstract principle hiding inside a contextualised question perform significantly better on contextualised assessments than students who encounter them cold.
q48v: These results suggest that subitizing is a low-key subconscious (neuroscientists call it preattentive) operation, while counting provokes significant cerebral activity. The eyes can immediately detect the difference between two and three items without counting.
q17: Chad Aldeman praises Fairfax County Public Schools for automatically enrolling more 8th graders in Algebra I Honors, but argues their parent communications undermined the initiative by focusing on risk and opt-out rather than celebrating student capability. He brings in communications expert Michelle Lerner to draft a model letter that personalizes the message, leads with congratulations, and frames acceleration as an opportunity rather than a burden. The revised letter centers the student by name, explains the value of early algebra for future coursework, and removes barriers to enrollment. The article demonstrates that even well-designed equity policies can be weakened by poor messaging, and that school districts owe families communication that matches the ambition of their programs. Aldeman's broader point is that messaging algebra acceleration as a default good—rather than a risky experiment—sends a fundamentally different signal about what schools believe students can do.
q49b: The agree/disagree statements will evoke emotions in most students. They also help them understand the concepts that are being shared. Advance organizers call on prior knowledge. Mental Note: Emotions take precedence over all other brain processes.
q1d: Chad Aldeman examines a mini-study of Khan Academy used as a supplemental math tool in 13 Newark, New Jersey schools over three years (approximately 6,700 students, 87% qualifying for free/reduced lunch). The results are notable on several fronts: 70% of students met the recommended usage threshold of 30 minutes daily -- far above the typical 'five percent problem' -- average math gains were small but positive, gains scaled linearly with engagement, and most strikingly, the largest gains accrued to the lowest-performing students. Aldeman argues this finding directly addresses post-pandemic achievement recovery, since low-performing students have been hardest hit. The key insight is not that Khan Academy is uniquely powerful, but that when engagement is high, even widely-criticized tools deliver measurable outcomes. Key Points * The 'five percent problem' -- the observation that most students don't engage with ed tech apps enough to see benefits -- was largely avoided: 70% of Newark students met recommended usage thresholds. * Returns were linear: students who used Khan Academy more and completed more lessons learned more -- confirming engagement as the fundamental variable in ed tech effectiveness. * Most important finding: the largest math gains by far were for level 1 (lowest-performing) students, directly addressing the group most severely affected by pandemic learning loss. * The study challenges reflexive ed tech dismissal by showing that the critical variable is implementation quality and actual use, not the technology's intrinsic power. * 87% of the 6,700 students qualified for free or reduced-price lunch -- making the engagement rates and outcomes especially notable for high-need populations. * Aldeman concludes: 'Schools and districts have tools available to address one of their biggest challenges. But students have to actually use those tools to see any benefits.'
q49t: Studying while seriously impaired is wasted time, in more ways than one, as millions of students have learned the hard way. Yet, generally speaking, we perform better on tests when in the same state of mind as when we studied. Moods, preoccupations, and perceptions matter, too: how we feel while studying, where we are, what we see and hear.
q1e: Chad Aldeman summarizes nine key takeaways from the updated Deans for Impact 'Science of Learning' report, translating cognitive science findings into practical implications for teachers, parents, and coaches. The report, developed with educational psychologists and now in its second edition, covers topics from prior knowledge and working memory limits to retrieval practice, feedback, and the persistence of myths like learning styles. Aldeman's synthesis highlights that strategies producing the strongest long-term retention -- spaced practice, retrieval, interleaving -- feel harder and less satisfying in the moment, creating a systematic bias toward less effective study approaches. He also notes that motivation need not precede learning: success often generates motivation, meaning teachers can build engagement through well-designed instruction rather than manufactured interest.
q4a8: We're interested in her jump school training because it's a great example of how some difficulties that elicit more effort and that slow down learning—spacing, interleaving, mixing up practice, and others—will more than compensate for their inconvenience by making the learning stronger, more precise, and more enduring. Short-term impediments that make for stronger learning have come to be called desirable difficulties.
q1m: Chad Aldeman identifies an internal contradiction in AFT President Randi Weingarten's AI-in-education speech, which simultaneously called for more project-based learning AND stronger foundational literacy, numeracy, and civic knowledge -- ideas that are in tension, not complementary. Aldeman argues that the progressive education argument ('facts are cheap, look them up') has been taken to its logical extreme by AI, which can now look up everything instantly -- and this actually makes foundational knowledge more important, not less. Drawing on Willingham's 2020 research in the AFT's own publication, Aldeman argues that domain knowledge is what enables critical thinking: abstract critical-thinking skills disconnected from content knowledge cannot substitute for actual fluency. The students who will benefit most from AI are those who know enough to ask good questions, evaluate answers, and build on what they learn -- not those who know the least.
q4aw: Creative instruction can be used to promote achievement across content areas, establish long-term learning, encourage creative thinking and problem solving, and foster motivation and engagement. Creative thinking lessons build on critical thinking and go beyond simple recall to consider "what if" possibilities and incorporate real-life problem solving; they require students to use both divergent and convergent thinking.
q1w: Homework rates in American schools have undergone dramatic swings over the past four decades, and Chad Aldeman uses longitudinal NCES survey data to trace those swings and interrogate their implications for student achievement. Among 9-year-olds, the percentage reporting no homework fell sharply from 35% in 1984 to 18% by 2008 — and then reversed, rising back to 34% by the most recent data, while the percentage completing one-plus hours of homework per night also declined substantially. Thirteen-year-olds have followed a steadier but similarly downward trajectory. Aldeman connects these trends to a broader erosion of academic accountability: as states have weakened graduation standards, reduced school accountability requirements, and responded to equity critiques of homework, expectations on students have quietly collapsed. The troubling correlation is that homework levels and math achievement scores moved in parallel — rising together through the 1990s and falling together thereafter, with the steepest declines concentrated among the lowest achievers. While careful not to assert direct causation from correlational data, Aldeman argues that this parallel is hard to ignore and that the evidence supports some homework — particularly the reinforcement variety — as producing genuine learning gains and better habit formation. He points to modern digital platforms like ASSISTments, Zearn, and Khan Academy as examples of how homework can be redesigned for the current era rather than simply eliminated.
q4bi: Learning requires time, effort, and motivation. The necessary ingredients are (a) time, (b) goal-orientation, (c) supportive feedback, (d) accumulated successful practice, and (e) frequent review. Notions such as instant experts, superfast learning, and other magic-like programs amount to faddish quackery in violation of known and validated principles of human learning.
q28: Chad Aldeman examines Fairfax County Public Schools' automatic-enrollment policy for algebra acceleration, which projects 84.5% of 8th graders in Algebra I or higher by next year -- far above national averages. Using Virginia SOL end-of-course exam data, he shows that pass rates are high across accelerated cohorts: 91.9% for 8th-grade Algebra I takers, 98.9% for 7th-graders, and 100% for 6th-graders. Aldeman invites guest commenter Pamela Hobart to explain why automatic opt-out enrollment policies are superior to recommendation-based systems: subjective gating leaves too much room for teacher bias and anti-acceleration defaults, particularly harming girls and students of color. Research cited by Hobart finds that students who don't take Algebra 1 by 8th grade rarely catch up -- top scorers who took it as 8th-graders were 3x to 55x more likely to pursue chemistry and physics than equally capable peers who waited. Aldeman's verdict: Fairfax is doing something clearly right, but the district's opt-out letter subtly undermines the policy by framing acceleration as a risk rather than an opportunity. Key Points * Fairfax County projects 84.5% of 8th graders in Algebra I or higher by next school year, with Virginia's statewide end-of-course exams providing an accountability backstop on quality. * Pass rates strongly suggest districts are underaccelerating: 91.9% of 8th-grade Algebra takers passed, 98.9% of 7th-graders, and 100% of 6th-graders -- with almost certainly some selection effects. * Automatic opt-out policies remove teacher anti-acceleration bias; subjective recommendation systems rarely self-correct because it's more noticeable when a student is moved up than quietly kept behind. * Students who don't take Algebra 1 by 8th grade rarely catch up: top scorers accelerated to 8th-grade algebra were 3x to 55x more likely to take chemistry and physics than equally capable peers who waited. * Fairfax's opt-out letter subtly undermines the spirit of Virginia's HB2868 by framing the accelerated placement as a 'risky bet requiring sophisticated discernment' rather than earned opportunity.
q4c8: Let us be clear: surface learning is not shallow learning. It is not about rote skills and meaningless algorithms. It is not prioritizing superficial learning or low-level skills over higher order skills. Surface learning begins with developing conceptual understanding, and then, at the right time, labels and procedures are explicitly introduced to give structure to concepts.
q2k: A nuanced critique of Engelmann's Direct Instruction framework arguing that while his theory of instructional communication is powerful for well-defined domains, it only solves the problem of how to present material clearly. The harder work—deciding what knowledge to teach, how to decompose it into teachable units, which simplifications to accept, and how to build narrative structures—happens upstream of Engelmann's framework. Allen distinguishes between domains where knowledge decomposes cleanly (early literacy, arithmetic) where Engelmann excels, and more contextual domains (history, economics) where curriculum design remains the primary intellectual challenge.
q4d2: This allows the teacher to tailor the lesson, so that the student can bridge the gap between his or her current knowledge and understanding, and the target knowledge and understanding. The primary concern is to add value to all students, wherever they start from.
q2p: Allen argues that the core problem in education improvement is not a shortage of instructional ideas -- AI has only multiplied those -- but a verification problem: we lack cheap, reliable ways to test which of many plausible instructional decisions actually produce better learning. Every instructional choice (splitting vs. inspection for factoring, planetary vs. energy-level model for atoms, sequential vs. comparative history teaching) is a wager on things we cannot directly observe, and the profession has been bad at discovering and preserving what works. Allen's conditional optimism is that LLM-supported online teaching may finally make it economically viable to test thousands of small instructional decisions at scale and gradually build a more cumulative science of how particular ideas are best taught. This is not an argument that LLMs possess pedagogical theory or that online testing reduces education to optimisation -- it is the modest claim that the economics of educational verification may be changing. Allen is careful to distinguish her position from naive enthusiasm: the verification problem is real and cannot be wished away with dashboards or bigger models.
q4dq: This sense of control over one's learning has been often studied. Students who take on personal responsibility for life events such as learning can be labeled internals. The notion that increasing achievement is a function of our efforts and interest is critical to success — there is no point, for example, in investing in study or preparation if we do not believe that our efforts can make a difference.
q2w: Becky Allen examines the question of whether AI-generated video can improve learning, and uses it as a case study in how educators mistake plausible mechanisms for actual explanations. Drawing on David Deutsch's criterion that a good explanation must be 'hard to vary' — its components cannot be changed without breaking the explanation — Allen argues that most claims about educational technology offer stories that are easy to vary, and therefore are not genuine explanations at all. She applies Höffler and Leutner's meta-analysis, which found a strong effect of animation for spatial/procedural content (d=1.06) but near-zero effects for declarative content, to show that 'video is engaging' explains nothing. She closes with a warning about the fluency trap: as AI lowers the production cost of educational video, the volume of plausible-but-untested material will increase, and educators will need stronger epistemic standards to resist it.
q4ee: The actual words we know are tags or labels for our packets of knowledge. Thus it makes intuitive sense that the more words we have, the more packets of knowledge, and, hence, the more background knowledge we have. The understanding that a word is the representation for a packet of knowledge enhances our understanding of vocabulary and greatly expands its usefulness.
q31: Teaching backwards—focused remediation through dialogue rather than curriculum chunks—works fundamentally differently from initial classroom instruction. Drawing from her experience as both classroom teacher and private tutor, Becky Allen demonstrates that effective gap-filling operates outside curriculum structures entirely. The critical insight is that tutoring succeeds because it works from the student's distributed understanding of their misconception, meeting their gap on its own terms rather than routing it through predetermined curriculum sequences. Modern language models are architecturally better suited to this dialogue-based diagnostic approach than traditional adaptive learning platforms, which force errors back through curriculum-shaped pipes. The optimal remediation space lies between small conceptual holes (best served by flexible dialogue) and massive gaps (which require structured initial instruction); for the middle range, conversational AI tutoring shows promise of doubling learning impact compared to human tutoring alone.
q4f2: Fear is an intense emotional response that shuts down higher cognitive processes so the brain can focus on the source of the fear. One of the greatest obstacles to learning is the fear of making mistakes. Because many students equate making mistakes with feeling humiliated, they will avoid learning tasks that appear too challenging. Effective teachers know that to prevent this, it is best to openly address these fears with students.
q35: Becky Allen argues, drawing on David Deutsch's The Beginning of Infinity, that educational constructs like 'understanding,' 'fluency,' and 'misconception' are genuinely real abstractions—not because they can be directly observed, but because they are part of our best explanations for patterns of learner behavior. She opens with the central problem: identical visible performances—the same correct answer, the same well-phrased claim—can be produced by entirely different underlying capacities (retrieval, calculation, genuine understanding), and surface performance alone cannot distinguish between them. Deutsch's standard—that abstractions are real when they are part of our best explanations, like software or money—rescues educational constructs from the crude objection that they are unreal because invisible, while simultaneously raising the standard for using them.
