Key Points * When a school has no designed Communication System, the principal becomes one by default -- creating an information environment where proximity to the principal determines what staff know, and the gap fills with rumor and fear. * The Weekly Rhythm (Wednesday leadership meeting + Friday staff email) ensures the hallway question never has to happen: the answer was already in the email, the decision was already in the meeting. * The Communication Cascade -- leaders briefed before staff, staff before families -- protects leaders' ability to steward the message rather than receive it alongside their teams. * The Principal's Voice is what only the principal can say: vision, hard truths, change announced in language that holds. It loses weight when deployed too often; restraint is the design choice that protects it. * Feedback Architecture designs upward channels rather than leaving them informal -- the 24/48 rule: if still bothering you 24 hours later, address directly with that person within 48 hours; after that, let it go.
q3b0: Using sandhill cranes' migratory cycles as a metaphor, Regenerative Schools argues that June reflection is the most important end-of-year task an educator or school leader can perform -- and the one they should never cut even when everything else is being pruned. The article provides a structured reflection protocol -- Toss, Tweak, Treasure -- from the Institute for Social and Emotional Learning, along with specific prompt categories for school leaders: pruning the task list, building buffers for thinking, culture of well-being, trust-building, collaboration, and culture of agency. It then argues for vulnerable transparency: sharing some of what you discovered in reflection with your team. Leaders who model hard work and rest, reflection and transparency, build regenerative schools that can sustain improvement across cycles.
q3j1: A reader's question -- whether some people naturally think in systems -- sent Nicole of The Critical Path into a research deep-dive on the cognitive origins of systems thinking. She finds that humans are born with causal curiosity and pattern-recognition instincts (the raw ingredients of systems thinking) but argues these are not the same as systems thinking itself. The key distinction is between holistic thinking (a natural tendency to sense relationships) and systems thinking (a disciplined practice that can map, test, and act on those relationships). Research on children's causal reasoning and relational reasoning suggests that the ability to transfer patterns across situations develops gradually with age, hinting that systems thinking can be built. The article concludes that treating systems thinking as a personality trait wastes its potential, while treating it as a teachable capability transforms how teams diagnose recurring project problems.
q9i: The debate between procedural fluency and conceptual understanding has stalled because neither side has a precise vocabulary for what a concept actually is. Drawing on Tiemann and Markle's taxonomy, the article defines a concept as a mental category with Critical Attributes (must-haves) and Variable Attributes (can-haves): concept learning has occurred only when a student can correctly classify a completely novel, unseen instance. This framework clarifies the most common instructional error—the proceduralization of concepts—which occurs when a concept-teaching tool like a bar model is turned into a mandatory drawing algorithm, teaching a new procedure rather than the underlying category. It also clarifies valid concept assessment: tests that ask students to respond to new instances, not reproduce familiar diagrams. The Instructional Hierarchy of Haring and Eaton provides the developmental sequence: concept acquisition must precede fluency, which precedes generalization; collapsing these stages produces the problems both sides of the debate diagnose. Procedural and conceptual understanding are genuinely different cognitive architectures requiring different instructional designs, and the Tiemann-Markle framework makes both teachable and assessable with precision. KEY TAKEAWAYS * A concept is a mental category defined by Critical Attributes (must-haves) and Variable Attributes (can-haves); concept learning occurs only when a student classifies novel, unseen instances correctly. * Proceduralizing concepts—turning a concept-teaching tool into a mandatory step-by-step routine—teaches a new procedure, not the underlying category. * Valid concept assessment asks students to respond to new instances, not define or reproduce familiar diagrams; the latter tests only memorized verbal algorithms. * Procedural fluency and conceptual understanding are different cognitive architectures requiring different instructional designs—conflating them is the source of most classroom confusion in this debate. * The Instructional Hierarchy requires acquisition to precede fluency, which precedes generalization—collapsing these stages produces exactly the problems both sides of the debate are trying to solve.
qc7: Literacy and reading are not the same thing. Most people are taught to decode symbols into meaning but not to read in the fuller sense, which involves progressive mastery across Mortimer Adler's four levels: Elementary, Inspectional, Analytical, and Syntopical. The article argues that treating reading as a binary skill—you either can or cannot—rather than a developable complex skill causes three predictable harms: people conclude they dislike reading when they are simply not yet skilled enough; they plateau because they believe there is nothing more to develop; and they mistake volume for depth, exemplified by listening to hundreds of books at 2.5x speed and calling it reading. Like chess or surgery, the skill of reading admits degrees of mastery that most people never approach—and most do not realize this. The good news is that recognizing reading as a cultivatable skill reverses all three harms, opening a path toward a richer reading life built through discipline, curiosity, and good practice. KEY TAKEAWAYS * Literacy (decoding symbols) and reading in the full analytical sense are different skills; most people are taught the former and never develop the latter. * Adler's four-level hierarchy—Elementary, Inspectional, Analytical, Syntopical—frames reading as a progressively complex skill at which most people plateau at level one without realizing it. * Treating reading as binary rather than developmental causes people to conclude they dislike it when they are simply not yet skilled enough to experience its rewards. * The volume-hype reading scene—hundreds of books per year at 2.5x speed—mistakes breadth for depth and passive consumption for active, analytical engagement. * Recognizing reading as a cultivatable skill reverses all three harms: reframes frustration as a growth stage, reveals the plateau, and redirects attention from volume to quality.
qne: Final post in the 16-part teaching wellbeing series. Teacher burnout is not about lacking resilience but about structurally unsustainable work. Sustainability means designing work humans can do well over time: bounded expectations, systems that reduce friction, protected recovery time. AI can help if used to reduce repetition and offload low-value tasks, not to accelerate pace. Real change requires system design and leadership modeling, not individual mindset shifts.
qr7: Education reformers are experiencing nostalgia for Bush-Obama era bipartisan consensus on testing, choice, and accountability. Mike Petrilli notes that reform fatigue resulted from two decades of push-back and public exhaustion with high-stakes testing. However, Greene argues reform failed not from fatigue but because it didn't work. High-stakes testing narrowed curriculum, damaged schools, and produced no academic gains while serving data-tech overlords and privatization advocates. True reform would ask how to make schools better, not how to inject market mechanisms. Including actual teachers in reform conversations would ground proposals in classroom reality.
qri: Greene traces the decline of whole-book reading in American schools to structural forces: Common Core's skill-heavy frame and testing regimes that reward speed over depth. When reading is treated as a transferable skill operable on any text, short passages become cheaper and easier to align; the result is steady substitution of excerpts for books. Whole books uniquely allow characters, ideas, and language to develop, train sustained attention, and invite reader-response conversations that require shared imaginative worlds. Greene extends the case to a broader principle of slow school: mathematics benefits from time with hard problems, history from long primary sources, writing from drafts that mature across weeks. The call is to resist speed-maximising assumptions and protect the experiences that make educated people.
qs8: Teachers fall into two pedagogical orientations: architects who design comprehensive knowledge structures and integrators who weave knowledge across contexts. Both have pedagogical merit. Architects build systematic, sequenced understanding. Integrators connect learning to real-world contexts. Excellence requires recognizing both approaches and understanding when each serves students best.
qsg: Hart outlines how collaborative planning meetings can improve teaching quality when focused on substance: subject content sequencing, pedagogy, misconceptions, task design, and adaptation. He warns against tokenistic sharing and emphasizes planning at least two weeks ahead to allow time for team feedback. Leadership teams must strategically choose which subjects to examine in depth based on data and teacher needs. Hart provides mental models for both planning for other teachers (clarity, slide notes, plenty of practice, advance sharing) and preparing to teach lessons planned by others (seeking clarification, providing feedback, rehearsing explanations).
qsi: This piece reframes team meetings not as logistical necessities but as primary mechanisms through which leadership actually happens in schools. Rather than viewing meetings as forums for sharing information and moving through agendas, the author argues they simultaneously accomplish six overlapping forms of work: establishing direction, achieving alignment across roles and timescales, constructing shared understanding of problems, enabling commitment to action, making decisions with clear accountability, and building psychological safety. Ineffective meetings actively undermine school culture by narrowing attention and fragmenting understanding; productive meetings increase the quality of attention across the organization. The framework shifts the leader's role toward shaping interactions that enable better conversations and decisions to emerge.
qsn: Nick Hart argues that well-structured team meetings are among the most powerful and underutilised tools for school improvement. Drawing on his experience as a school leader, Hart identifies nine distinct functions that effective team meetings serve: developing subject knowledge, providing pedagogical advice, refining planning, advancing strategic priorities, focusing on vulnerable children, debriefing decisions, standardising expectations, managing communication and logistics, and conducting quality assurance. Most school teams use meetings for only one or two of these functions — typically logistics and communication — while the high-leverage functions like subject knowledge development and pedagogical practice are rarely addressed. Hart argues that school improvement is a collective professional development problem, and team meetings are the infrastructure through which collective development happens — or fails to.
quj: Frederick Hess, drawing on 35 years of observation from high school teacher to AEI fellow, warns the new generation of school reformers—energized by science-of-reading mandates, school choice expansion, and post-pandemic reckoning—not to repeat the mistakes of the Bush-Obama era. He identifies five lessons: don't mistake legislative wins for lasting victories (the real work follows), resist drinking the Kool-Aid (true believers dismiss legitimate criticism), listen with genuine purpose rather than performing engagement, treat money as a mixed blessing rather than a solution, and recognize that six-sevenths of reform is invisible to outsiders. Written with candor about past failures—Reading First, teacher evaluation, Common Core—he argues that 'reform done poorly is often worse than no reform at all' and that today's reformers face the additional challenge of polarized echo chambers and algorithm-driven social media that make escape from tribal thinking harder than ever.
qz3: Cultural pressure to prevent the summer slide leads parents and teachers to push children into academic catch-up work, often producing the opposite of the intended effect. The article argues that learning requires oscillation between challenge and confidence: always operating at the cognitive edge is exhausting and counterproductive, and children need summer to develop genuine fluency with what they already know before building on it. The master carpenter analogy is illuminating: roughly 85% of expert performance involves mastered core skills executed with ease, with only 15% at the growth edge—and that challenge is satisfying precisely because of the competent foundation beneath it. The case of Jessie, a tenth-grader testing at fourth-grade level, confirms the principle: when given permission to work within her comfort zone for months through graphic novels, low-stakes math games, and unstructured time, her foundations solidified, her comfort zone expanded naturally, and she moved through subsequent academic material faster than expected. Summer is best used not for advancement but for fluency-building through project-based, self-directed work that applies existing skills in contexts children own. The prescription is simple: more unstructured time, project scaffolding without evaluation pressure, and the parental courage to step off the advancement treadmill. KEY TAKEAWAYS * Learning requires oscillation between challenge and confidence; always operating at the cognitive edge is exhausting and counterproductive for children and adults alike. * The master carpenter analogy: roughly 85% of expert work involves mastered core skills, with only 15% at the growth edge—that challenge is satisfying because of the competent foundation underneath. * Summer is best used for fluency-building and confidence through project-based, self-directed work that applies existing academic skills in contexts the child owns. * Unstructured time combined with simple project scaffolding is the sweet spot: enough structure to sustain engagement, enough freedom to make the work feel like an adventure. * Allowing a struggling student to work within her comfort zone for months solidified her foundations, expanded her comfort zone naturally, and accelerated subsequent academic progress beyond expectation.
q14c: Reading fluency is better understood as a red carpet than a bridge: the carpet (fluency) must be crossed with ease and presence so that cognitive attention is available for the press line (vocabulary and background knowledge) and ultimately the award (comprehension). A reader still decoding at conscious effort has no capacity left to work the press line—and when the press line is dense with unfamiliar vocabulary, reporters step onto the carpet itself, slowing the crossing. The article's central reframe is that struggling readers are not broken—they are undertraveled: they have not walked enough carpets to build the automaticity that frees cognitive space for meaning-making. Fluency is also venue-specific; a reader fluent in narrative fiction may feel lost in a science text because domain vocabulary and background knowledge constitute different reporters in a different venue. The intervention is not drilling a single text through repetition but varied repetition across many texts and genres—more carpets, not more practice on the same one. The Simple View of Reading and Scarborough's Rope are not replaced by this framework but made visible and navigable. KEY TAKEAWAYS * The red carpet reframes fluency: the carpet is fluency, the press line is vocabulary and background knowledge, the award is comprehension—but only for readers who cross fully present. * Struggling readers are not broken; they are undertraveled—they have not walked enough carpets to develop the automaticity that frees cognitive space for meaning. * Fluency is venue-specific: a reader fluent in one text type may struggle completely in another because domain vocabulary and background knowledge are different reporters at a different carpet. * Transferable fluency requires varied repetition across many text types and domains—not repeated reading of a single text. * Wide, varied reading is not optional enrichment—it is the mechanism by which fluency generalizes until a reader can walk into almost any room and find their footing.
q154: Backward design, introduced by Wiggins and McTighe, flips the traditional order of instructional planning from content-first to outcome-first. Instead of choosing materials and activities and then assessing them, you begin by defining what learners should actually be able to do, then specify evidence of that learning, and only then select content and experiences that move toward that outcome. The pattern breaks the accident of 'ripe-with-information-but-light-on-impact' training. Martinez walks through the four anchors: outcome (what they should do differently), evidence (how you'll know they learned it), experience (which content or activities will help), and follow-up (what deepens learning after).
q172: Olivia Mullins describes her arc from enthusiastic NGSS adopter to sceptic, driven by her encounter with cognitive science research that undermines the framework's foundational assumptions. The NGSS's guided-inquiry model asks novice students to reason like scientists — to construct explanations from phenomena, engage in argumentation from evidence, and develop models. But as Daniel Willingham's research on the novice-expert distinction demonstrates, novices lack the organised prior knowledge that makes scientific reasoning possible; without it, open-ended inquiry generates confusion rather than learning. Mullins argues the model is particularly damaging for the most vulnerable learners, who have the least background knowledge to draw on and who benefit most from explicit instruction. She concludes that direct instruction combined with structured hands-on activity is more effective and more equitable than the guided-inquiry model NGSS promotes.
q19i: Mr. Nickhart examines the paradox at the heart of school improvement: schools are complex adaptive systems whose outcomes are unpredictable, yet experienced leaders navigate them partly through pattern recognition. He argues that improvement plans are necessarily written as if cause-and-effect are direct, but that this is a presentation convention, not a description of reality. The real problem arises when leaders begin to believe the neat narrative of their own plans. Drawing on systems thinking principles -- cause and effect are not closely related in time and space; the harder you push, the harder the system pushes back; easy fixes tend to loop back in -- he calls for better pattern recognition in school leaders rather than better plan templates. The article closes by framing June's Leadership Handbook post as an attempt to provide that practical pattern-recognition scaffolding.
q19t: Mr. Nickhart provides a worked example of how he structures school improvement strategy, organised around four foundational concepts: strategic intent, a cohesive hierarchy, culture, and continuous checking. He begins from Vivianne Robinson's caution that designing the future before understanding the present leads to misdirected resources. Strategic intent, in his school's case, is embodied in the 'horizon' metaphor -- continuous improvement toward a moving target -- and three 'horizons': a flourishing school, an evolving curriculum, and serving community. The article maps how strategy, systems, culture-setting, and capacity-building each decompose differently at SLT, subject leader, and year-leader levels, and argues that trust is the prior condition without which none of the above functions.
q1a4: O'Brien argues that most school culture systems fail because they are designed for one human — either the student or the adult — while burdening the other. Systems built exclusively for students produce aspirational expectations that exhaust adults who must improvise enforcement without tools or language. Systems built exclusively for adults produce clear procedures that manage students without forming them into community. The design move that makes culture systems hold is to explicitly name and serve both humans simultaneously, building experiences that reinforce rather than compete with each other. O'Brien walks through three concrete cases: tiered behavior systems, first days of school, and the reflection room — showing in each case what the student experience and the adult experience look like when designed in parallel versus designed in isolation. The test he offers any school leader is simple: for any given culture subsystem, can you answer without hesitation what the student is experiencing and what the adult is experiencing? Where the answers are vague or the experiences compete, the gap has been found and the redesign cycle should begin there.
q1af: Chris O'Brien argues that most summer leadership retreats produce aspiration without implementation, resulting in plans that quietly die by October. The article is a practical framework for principals who have already done the strategic work—set OKRs, named the destination—and now need to translate vision into operational reality before the school year begins. O'Brien structures summer work in three layers: first, aligning leadership teams by translating OKRs into specific systems, behaviors, and dated milestones with clear ownership; second, defining school culture with behavioral precision so that teachers can model and practice the exact routines they will run on Day 1; and third, designing the student experience backward from what a student will feel in the first ninety seconds of the school day. Throughout, he insists that leadership development happens through exposure to ambiguous problems and judgment work, not through decks and OKRs—and that the whole job of summer is to make September 'boring, predictable, designed.' The article is targeted at school leaders but contains transferable principles about the gap between strategy and implementation that apply to any organizational leadership context.
q1al: Chris O'Brien argues that principals who design excellent systems for every part of their school -- hiring, instruction, student culture, communication -- routinely fail to design the one system that determines whether the others survive: themselves. The hero principal operates on heroism and adrenaline, becoming a single point of failure; the systemic principal designs the role itself. O'Brien introduces a 'single point of failure' diagnostic: what breaks the day I'm out sick? The longer that list, the more the principal has become the system rather than building one. He then prescribes the 'Minimize Audit' -- sorting all your responsibilities into Column 1 (only-me work requiring your actual authority) versus Column 2 (only-me-for-now work that should be systematized). Column 2 is not your job; it is a list of systems you haven't built yet. The article argues that indispensability is fragility, and that the real work of a systemic leader is to empty Column 2 so the only work remaining is the high-leverage work that genuinely requires you.
q1e2: Poulsen offers a three-question framework for burned-out educators wrestling with whether to leave their current role. Drawing on Maslach and Leiter's research identifying six burnout sources (workload, control, reward, community, fairness, values mismatch), she argues that focusing only on overwork misses the actual problem in most cases. The first question -- what exactly is causing the burnout? -- requires specific diagnosis before any action makes sense. The second -- what is within your control? -- separates genuine influenceability from structural constraints. The third -- what is your next right step? -- uses Tracey Gee's insight that action leads to clarity to move educators out of rumination into movement, even if small. Poulsen also surfaces a third option beyond stay or go: a different lane in the same school, or the same role with a different supervisor. Research shows employees who feel administratively supported are 70% less likely to burn out -- environment is often more determinative than individual coping strategies. The essay ends with a call not to stay out of guilt or martyrdom.
q1eu: Using Maslach and Leiter's six-factor burnout framework -- workload, control, reward, community, fairness, and values -- the author offers three diagnostic questions for educators experiencing chronic stress: what specifically is causing the burnout, what is within their control, and what is their next right step. The piece argues that most burnout responses focus too narrowly on workload when the actual source may be values misalignment, lack of recognition, community breakdown, or unfairness -- and that misdiagnosing the source leads to ineffective cures. The author shares personal experience of burnout caused not by volume of work but by being repeatedly undermined and required to act against their own values -- naming this as moral injury. Drawing on Tracey Gee's principle that 'action leads to clarity,' the piece advises moving beyond the binary stay-or-go choice to consider a third option: a lateral move to a different role in the same school, or a different school with a more supportive supervisor, noting that employees who feel supported by their administration are 70% less likely to burn out.
q1gf: This is Part 3 in Salles's practical series on classroom behaviour, providing a toolbox of specific phrases and questions that any teacher can use immediately, regardless of charisma, relationship-building skill, or emotional self-regulation. The article is explicitly not a self-improvement guide — it does not ask teachers to become better people, become trauma-informed, or build deep relationships first — but instead provides a laminated set of tools that produce behavioural improvement from the first day. Salles organises the language into four phases: psychological safety framing (before students start), managing task performance and memory (redirecting during work), eliciting and upgrading thought (after initial responses), and feedback and redirection. The psychological safety language reframes mistakes as the expected and desired state: 'I'm not expecting anyone to get this right first time. That's how we learn' — removing the shame that makes students refuse to engage. The elicitation phase includes high-leverage sequences such as 'Yes, and...' and 'Who can say this even better?' that invite elaboration without threatening the initial respondent. Salles acknowledges the artificiality of some techniques (like tracking the speaker) but explains that what makes students genuinely listen is knowing their opinions matter and that they don't have to agree with the teacher.
q1gq: Drawing on Adam Grant's Giver/Taker/Matcher framework, Dominic Salles argues that school culture is shaped more decisively by who is hired than by any programme or initiative. A single Taker — someone who extracts value from colleagues rather than contributing it — can depress team output by 30–40%, a cost that dwarfs even the uplift from a top-performing Giver. Salles warns that the most dangerous hire is not the obvious Taker but the Agreeable Taker: the colleague who appears warm and collegial while quietly hoarding credit and offloading burden. Matchers, who balance giving and taking, tend to mirror the dominant norm — so in a Taker culture they become Takers too, and in a Giver culture they become Givers. The practical implication is that leaders must screen hard for Taker behaviour at the recruitment stage, because cultural remediation after the fact is costly and often fails. Salles closes by noting that the goal is not saintliness but a baseline of generosity that becomes self-sustaining once established.
q1gw: Dominic Salles responds to a Mark Enser post on school quality assurance by proposing a more structured alternative based on his experience leading school improvement. The central argument is that quality assurance too often becomes an exercise in reassurance rather than understanding -- learning walks drift toward surface features, book looks confirm compliance rather than growth, and data stands in for professional judgment -- because it is disconnected from a clear definition of what leaders actually need to know. Salles' preferred alternative is a precise checklist structure: not ambiguous criteria open to subjective interpretation, but specific, universal teaching behaviors that are known to produce learning gains, applied consistently across all subjects. He distinguishes between proxies for teaching and the actual goal (learning), arguing that a small set of well-chosen universal teaching routines -- his school used 'The Essential 8' -- creates conditions for subject leaders, coaches, and teachers to improve in a measurable, cumulative way. The article delivers four concrete checklists covering curriculum quality, lesson visits, book looks, and personal CPD cycles, each grounded in the principle that precision and focus are more powerful than rich but ambiguous frameworks.
q1h3: A comprehensive national survey of student attitudes reveals significantly low engagement across five critical domains: safety, behavior, academic value, enjoyment, and peer relationships. The data comes from the ImpactEd student voice assessment, a 32-question instrument that measures cognitive engagement (whether students feel safe, perceive academic value, and exercise agency), emotional engagement (enjoyment, worry levels, and sense of belonging), and behavioral engagement (effort and persistence). The findings are sobering: average engagement scores lag well behind what educators would hope. Schools can use this benchmark tool to situate their own student populations, celebrate areas where they exceed national averages, and identify specific improvement targets grounded in evidence. The survey provides a diagnostic window into which dimensions of student experience are strongest and which require intervention.
q1hq: Inductive learning moves from concrete examples to general rules; deductive learning moves from rules to examples. Traditional education is heavily deductive—rules first, examples second—which fails most learners because they are asked to engage with abstract principles before any concrete context has created a need for those principles. A 2011 meta-analysis by Alfieri et al. confirms that pure unguided discovery underperforms direct instruction (effect size d = -0.38), but enhanced inductive learning—with scaffolding, guidance, and explicit conclusions—outperforms it (d = +0.30). The article's central argument is that well-designed inductive learning is not open discovery but carefully curated guided discovery: a show-don't-tell-then-tell structure where selected examples engineer a need to know, and the explicit rule arrives as an answer to a question the learner is already holding. The generation effect amplifies retention—people remember information better when they predict or produce it before receiving the answer, even imperfectly. Practical design formulas (STP, PTP, MTE, CDT) all follow the same underlying logic: create tension or curiosity first, then resolve it with theory. Experts often resist this approach due to the expertise reversal effect, but this resistance reflects their own cognitive efficiency rather than optimal design for beginners. KEY TAKEAWAYS * Inductive learning (concrete to general) outperforms deductive learning (general to concrete) when properly guided—meta-analysis finds effect size d = +0.30 for enhanced inductive vs. traditional instruction. * Pure unguided discovery significantly underperforms (d = -0.38); the power is in guided, scaffolded discovery—good inductive design tells learners exactly where to look. * The need to know is a deliberate engineering goal: start with a carefully chosen example that makes the learner lean in before the rule is revealed. * The generation effect means learners remember information far better when they predict or produce it before receiving the correct version—even wrong guesses deepen retention. * Experts often resist inductive design due to the expertise reversal effect, mistaking their own cognitive efficiency for the optimal learning path for beginners.
q1nd: In this issue of Schooled, Mike Petrilli gives his 'Raise Your Hand' section to Randy Squier, a former New York superintendent, who cautions that 'Portrait of a Graduate' initiatives must complement rigorous academic standards rather than substitute for them. The broader issue also covers state recess mandates (Dale Chu's concern about substituting seat-time inputs for outcome thinking), the AI debate between David Wallace-Wells (skeptical) and Dan Weisberg (optimistic about teacher-support applications), and the civic education landscape. Larry Berger of Amplify explains why the 'science of math' is more complex than the science of reading, requiring both open problem-solving and explicit procedural instruction. Jason Furman and David Laibson defend Harvard's grade inflation cap, acknowledging its imperfections while arguing it solves a collective action problem.
q1ww: Karen Vaites examines the Thomas B. Fordham Institute's latest Science of Reading progress report and finds a familiar pattern: measurable gains in teacher knowledge have not translated into measurable gains in classroom practice. Teachers trained in UFLI-style structured literacy now know more about phonemic awareness and decoding — but Benchmark Assessment System remains on state-approved curriculum lists despite being classified as 'unscientific' by independent reviewers, and three-cueing persists in classrooms across 17 states that have technically banned it. Vaites argues the report is Exhibit 729 in the ongoing case that policy and progress are not the same thing. Passing a law changes the legal landscape; it does not change what a teacher does at 9:15 on a Tuesday. The article calls for a shift from policy advocacy to implementation science.
q1yd: Wexler examines Louisiana's Innovative Assessment (IA), an experiment grounding reading tests in taught content rather than generic skills. Though discontinued in 2024 after only two full years, the IA showed promise: students felt less anxious, teachers shifted from isolated skills to whole-text meaning-making, and gaps between disadvantaged and affluent students narrowed. However, changing state tests requires coordination across bureaucratic silos—curriculum officials, test designers, legislatures—and the political will rarely materializes. The article argues that what gets tested gets taught, making content-aligned assessment crucial for knowledge-building reform.
q1zg: Expeditionary education—learning through extended investigations and field-based experiences—offers a coherent alternative to traditional instruction. Rather than fragmented lessons, students engage in sustained projects that require applying knowledge from multiple domains to real investigations. The power of this approach lies not in the novelty of outdoor education but in the structural coherence: students learn mathematics because calculations are required for their expedition; they develop writing because documentation is necessary; they engage in science because investigation demands it. This intrinsic motivation combined with forced integration of knowledge creates learning conditions superior to both traditional classroom instruction and disconnected enrichment activities. However, expeditionary education requires different school organization and teacher preparation than conventional schooling.
q1zm: Effort itself can be rewarding, but schools have systematically removed conditions that make effort enjoyable and replaced them with meaningless difficulty. Students work hardest when their effort produces visible progress toward goals they care about—solving problems, building understanding, creating something valuable. When schools extract effort from purpose—asking students to work hard on meaningless tasks—they disconnect effort from its psychological reward. The result is students who avoid effort and schools that interpret this as laziness rather than rational response to poor incentive structures. Reconnecting effort to meaningful outcome requires redesigning what students work on, not simply demanding more motivation.
q24e: The feedback is one of the most common pieces of 360-review language a fast-moving leader receives, and the authors make the useful move of splitting it in two. There are actually two distinct problems hiding inside "you don't bring people along." The first is communicating the rationale behind the leader's thinking and task requests. The second is gaining subscription to the resulting plan, usually through inclusion in the work of shaping it. The piece focuses on the first because subscription has been covered in earlier Field Notes, and because the communication failure is the one that most often catches high-capacity leaders by surprise. The diagnostic is sharp. Facile leaders who, like sharks, are in constant motion tend to run several steps ahead of the team before anyone else has seen the problem. That speed is a genuine strength until the leader fails to stop and say what they are seeing, what strategy they are forming, and what reasoning underlies the requests being handed down. Team members asked to execute on conclusions they do not understand either execute without rigor or intentionally drag their feet because the absence of explanation reads as disrespect. Over time, uncertainty becomes disengagement and rigor drops out of the work. The corrective is two-part and deceptively simple. Tell the team what you are seeing, what you are thinking, and why, before any plan takes shape. Then invite them to probe the evidence and the reasoning rather than debate the conclusions. The move is not to surrender the decision, it is to open the working assumptions to challenge from the people who will have to execute against them. Good leaders bring people along by making their reasoning audible and inspectable, which is the work that closes the gap between a fast leader and a team that can actually keep pace.
q2si: Nick Hart extends his three-layer school improvement model -- base layer (horizon habits), middle layer (improvement plan), top layer (strategic direction) -- to argue that action planning is the artefact of the middle layer only, and that leaders working at the base layer should be building systems and timelines rather than writing plans. At the base layer, leaders are reinforcing established ways of working, addressing within-school variance, and prioritising coherence over consistency -- ensuring that shared principles are applied thoughtfully across different contexts rather than demanding identical practice. Hart argues that most teacher learning is informal, happening through day-to-day collaboration rather than formal courses, so the artefact that matters most at the base layer is a timeline that helps leaders organise and prioritise collaborative activity. He illustrates this with a worked example for a Head of Year, contrasting two approaches to planning meetings: one that delegates planning upstream and then asks 'any questions?' and one that structures the meeting to build collective knowledge through guided discussion of specific decisions. The article is addressed to school leaders who default to action-planning for every problem regardless of which layer of improvement work is actually required, arguing that the act of writing an action plan can itself become a substitute for the more important work of building the habits and collaborative structures that improvement actually requires.
q2vq: Jolein, a district-level education leader, argues that the conventional career ladder in education -- from teacher to coach to assistant principal to principal to district leader -- is a dangerous mental model that equates upward movement with advancement and proximity to students with regression. The piece opens with the author's own admission that after years away from school buildings, they had lost sharpness -- and instead of hiding from it, they increased their reps (covering schools, being present in buildings). The core concept is 'muscular humility' (Daniel Coyle): a leadership posture that finds simple ways to serve the group and signals 'we are all in this together,' insisting 'I am not above practicing.' The ladder makes leaders brittle: when identity is tied only to one role or salary band, any disruption becomes existential, producing leaders who protect their image rather than tell the truth. The essay calls for leaders to build identities that work across roles -- to find worth in the work itself rather than in the title.
